expansion_prompt: Break down the core infrastructure provisioning into parallel work streams: (1) Namespace and RBAC setup, (2) CloudNative-PG cluster deployment and schema initialization Job, (3) Opstree Valkey deployment, (4) Kubernetes Secrets creation for all external APIs, (5) Signal-CLI pod and PVC deployment, (6) Cloudflare R2 bucket configuration and ConfigMap aggregation, (7) ArgoCD Application CR and validation. Implementer subagents should work on datastores (2, 3) in parallel after namespaces are ready. Secret creation (4) can proceed independently. Validation subagent should verify all pods reach Running state, schemas are created, and ConfigMap keys are populated. Note dependency ordering: namespaces must exist before any other resource; schema init Job depends on CNPG cluster being Ready.
reasoning: This task spans 9 distinct infrastructure components across 4 namespaces with tight dependency ordering. Involves CloudNative-PG operator CRs, post-init Jobs with SQL DDL, Opstree Redis CRs, Signal-CLI with PVC, 9 Kubernetes Secrets, a ConfigMap aggregating dynamic connection strings, and ArgoCD GitOps wiring. Each component has its own failure mode and validation step. The schema creation Job and per-service role grants add SQL orchestration complexity on top of Kubernetes resource management. High risk of cross-cutting dependency failures during validation.

expansion_prompt: Break down the Equipment Catalog Service into parallel work streams: (1) Cargo workspace initialization, dependency setup, and database migration files for catalog schema tables, (2) Core CRUD handlers for categories and products with JWT middleware, (3) Availability calculation logic with overlap query and unit tests, (4) Valkey sliding-window rate limiter middleware, (5) AI agent machine-readable API endpoints and programmatic checkout endpoint, (6) Prometheus metrics, health endpoints, and GDPR internal endpoints, (7) Kubernetes Deployment manifest, Dockerfile, and integration test suite. Streams 2, 3, 4 can proceed in parallel once stream 1 is complete. Stream 5 depends on stream 3. Tester subagent should validate cargo-tarpaulin coverage >= 80%, p99 availability check < 500ms, and rate limiter 429 behavior.
reasoning: Full Rust/Axum microservice from scratch with 11 distinct handler groups, custom availability overlap calculation, Valkey rate limiting with async connection pooling, JWT middleware, Prometheus instrumentation, S3/R2 integration, GDPR endpoints, and Kubernetes deployment. The combination of Rust's type system demands, async SQLx migrations, and the performance SLO (<500ms p99) adds significant implementation and testing complexity. The AI agent API and programmatic checkout endpoint require careful availability atomicity handling.

task_title: Build Rental Management System Service (Grizz - Go/gRPC)
expansion_prompt: Break down the RMS service into parallel work streams: (1) Go module initialization, protobuf definitions in proto/sigma1/rms/v1/ with buf generate, and grpc-gateway setup, (2) Database migrations for all 7 rms schema tables, (3) OpportunityService and ProjectService gRPC handlers with REST gateway mapping, (4) InventoryService with conflict detection logic and unit tests, (5) CrewService and DeliveryService handlers, (6) ScoreLead algorithm with external vetting service HTTP call and unit tests, (7) Google Calendar integration on project confirmation, (8) Prometheus metrics, health endpoints on separate mux, GDPR endpoints, and Kubernetes Deployment. Streams 3, 4, 5 can proceed in parallel once streams 1 and 2 are complete. Stream 6 depends on stream 3. Stream 7 depends on stream 3. Tester subagent should verify gRPC reflection, conflict detection 409 response, and GDPR anonymization.
reasoning: Most complex single service in the platform: combines gRPC + protobuf code generation, grpc-gateway REST bridge on separate ports, 7 database tables across 5 domain areas, 5 distinct gRPC service definitions with 15+ methods, a cross-service HTTP call for ScoreLead, Google Calendar OAuth2 integration, inventory conflict detection requiring atomic transaction logic, separate health mux to avoid gateway conflicts, and GDPR anonymization. The dual-protocol exposure (gRPC :9090 + REST :8080 + health :8081) and external API dependencies make this architecturally the most involved service.

expansion_prompt: Break down the Finance Service into parallel work streams: (1) Cargo workspace initialization, dependencies, and database migrations for all 5 finance schema tables, (2) Invoice CRUD handlers with tax calculation logic and status state machine, (3) Payment recording handlers and Stripe invoice creation/webhook endpoint with signature validation, (4) Financial report endpoints (revenue, aging buckets, cashflow, profitability), (5) Payroll entry handlers, currency rate sync background task with Valkey caching, (6) Automated overdue reminder background task and JWT middleware, (7) Prometheus metrics, health endpoints, GDPR endpoints, and Kubernetes Deployment. Streams 2, 5 can proceed in parallel once stream 1 is complete. Stream 3 depends on stream 2 for invoice_id references. Stream 4 depends on streams 2 and 3. Tester subagent should verify aging bucket correctness, Stripe webhook signature validation, and background task state transitions.
reasoning: Rust/Axum service with significant financial domain complexity: 5 schema tables, Stripe webhook integration with HMAC signature validation, two tokio background tasks (currency sync every 6h, overdue reminder daily), 4 complex reporting queries (aging buckets require date arithmetic), multi-currency support with Valkey caching layer, tax calculation logic, and a complete invoice payment state machine. The combination of async background tasks, external Stripe API integration, and financial correctness requirements elevates this above a standard CRUD service.

expansion_prompt: Break down the Customer Vetting Service into parallel work streams: (1) Cargo workspace initialization, dependencies, and database migrations for vetting schema tables, (2) POST /api/v1/vetting/run handler with tokio::spawn pipeline orchestration and status tracking, (3) OpenCorporates and LinkedIn API integration steps with 10s per-step timeout and error handling, (4) Google Reviews Places API integration and weighted scoring algorithm with unit tests (scoring logic is independently testable), (5) Valkey 24h result caching and GET /api/v1/vetting/:org_id retrieval endpoint, (6) JWT middleware, Prometheus metrics, health endpoints, GDPR delete/export endpoints, and Kubernetes Deployment. Streams 3 and 4 can be developed in parallel once stream 2's pipeline scaffold exists. Tester subagent should verify 202 async response, polling convergence, cache hit behavior, and GDPR deletion 404 follow-up.
reasoning: Multi-step async background pipeline with 4 external API integrations (OpenCorporates, LinkedIn OAuth2, Google Places, credit stub), each with independent timeout and failure handling. The weighted scoring algorithm, Valkey result caching with TTL, and the two-table status tracking (request + result) add meaningful complexity. However, compared to Finance and RMS, there are fewer tables, no background scheduled tasks, and simpler state transitions. The main complexity driver is the resilient async pipeline orchestration with partial failure semantics.

expansion_prompt: Break down the Social Media Engine into parallel work streams: (1) Project initialization, TypeScript config, database migrations for social schema tables, and Effect.Service/Schema type definitions, (2) Photo upload handler, R2 multipart upload integration, and draft creation with status tracking, (3) AI curation pipeline: OpenAI vision scoring, sharp image processing/cropping for 4 platform formats, R2 upload of cropped versions, (4) Caption generation via OpenAI chat API with event context assembly, (5) Platform publishing Effect services for Instagram, LinkedIn, TikTok, Facebook with exponential backoff retry and timeout, (6) Approval workflow endpoint, Signal notification trigger, and portfolio GET endpoint, (7) JWT middleware, Prometheus metrics, health endpoints, GDPR endpoints, and Kubernetes Deployment. Streams 3 and 4 can proceed in parallel once stream 2 is complete. Stream 5 is independent and can be developed in parallel. Tester subagent should verify Effect retry behavior with mocked failures and platform publish status recording.
reasoning: Complex Node.js/Elysia service combining novel stack choices (Effect 3.x functional patterns, Elysia Bun-native framework), sharp image processing with 4 platform-specific crop formats, OpenAI vision API integration for photo scoring, multi-platform social media API publishing with 4 different API contracts, Effect.retry with exponential backoff, an approval workflow with Signal notification, and R2 storage integration. The Effect programming model adds architectural complexity beyond typical Node.js services.

expansion_prompt: Break down Morgan AI Agent configuration into parallel work streams: (1) OpenClaw agent YAML, system prompt, and skill manifests for all 9 skill areas, (2) MCP tools.yaml configuration for all 12 tools with correct in-cluster service URLs, request/response schemas, and tool descriptions, (3) Signal-CLI adapter configuration with JSON-RPC connection and receive loop, (4) ElevenLabs voice configuration and Twilio SIP trunk TwiML webhook setup, (5) Kubernetes Deployment in openclaw namespace with PVC, envFrom secrets, and Cloudflare Tunnel annotation for Signal webhook, (6) End-to-end tool connectivity testing: one request per MCP tool in interactive CLI mode, Signal message receipt verification, and web chat endpoint validation. Streams 1, 2, 3, 4 can proceed in parallel. Stream 5 depends on streams 1-4 being complete. Stream 6 depends on all 5 backend services being Running (tasks 2-6).
reasoning: Configuration-heavy task spanning 4 distinct integration surfaces (MCP tools, Signal-CLI, ElevenLabs/Twilio voice, web chat), 12 MCP tool definitions each requiring correct URL patterns, authentication handling, and response parsing, plus 9 skill manifests with embedded decision logic. The GDPR orchestration tools (sigma1_gdpr_export and sigma1_gdpr_delete) must fan out to 5 services and aggregate results. Complexity is primarily in the breadth of integrations and the correctness of tool-to-service wiring rather than algorithmic depth.

expansion_prompt: Break down the Sigma-1 Website into parallel work streams: (1) Next.js 15 project initialization, shared design token package, Tailwind 4 config, shadcn/ui setup, and root layout with sidebar/mobile navigation, (2) Effect ApiClient service, Effect.Schema type definitions for all backend response shapes, and TanStack Query integration layer, (3) Equipment catalog page and product detail page with real-time availability date picker using TanStack Query, (4) Quote builder page with Effect.Schema validation and RMS opportunity submission, portfolio page with masonry grid and lightbox, (5) Morgan web chat widget embedding, AI-native routes (llms.txt, llms-full, Schema.org JSON-LD), (6) Cloudflare Pages deployment config (wrangler.toml, next-on-pages, GitHub Actions workflow), Playwright E2E test suite, and Lighthouse CI integration. Streams 3 and 4 can proceed in parallel once streams 1 and 2 are complete. Stream 5 is partially independent (chat widget depends on Task 7, llms.txt routes do not).
reasoning: Full Next.js 15 App Router application with novel stack elements (Effect 3.x in React context, TailwindCSS 4, React 19 patterns), shared design token package, 7 distinct route implementations, real-time availability UI requiring TanStack Query integration, Effect.Schema validation on forms, Morgan web chat widget embedding from a Kubernetes service, AI-native llms.txt routes, and Cloudflare Pages deployment pipeline. The combination of new framework versions (Next 15, React 19, TW4) and Effect integration in a server/client hybrid architecture adds meaningful complexity.

task_title: Build Sigma-1 Mobile App (Tap - Expo/React Native)
expansion_prompt: Break down the Sigma-1 Mobile App into parallel work streams: (1) Expo project initialization, NativeWind 4 setup with shared design token import, Expo Router file-based structure, and bottom tab navigator layout, (2) Effect data fetching layer (lib/api.ts) with Effect.Schema type definitions, TanStack Query setup, and shared API client, (3) Equipment catalog tab with FlatList, search/debounce, expo-image for R2 URLs, and product detail screen with DateRangePicker and availability query, (4) Quote builder tab with Effect.Schema form validation and opportunity submission, Morgan chat tab with native WebSocket/EventSource or WebView implementation, (5) Portfolio tab with FlashList masonry grid and full-screen modal, barcode scanner screen with expo-camera, push notification setup with Expo Push Token registration, (6) EAS Build configuration (eas.json profiles), CI pipeline integration, and Jest/RNTL test suite with >= 80% coverage. Streams 3 and 4 can proceed in parallel once streams 1 and 2 are complete. Stream 5 is partially independent.
reasoning: Cross-platform React Native app with NativeWind 4 (which internally still uses TW3, adding configuration friction), Expo Router file-based navigation, Effect 3.x data layer, 5 distinct tab screens, custom DateRangePicker component, expo-camera barcode scanning, Expo Push Notification integration requiring backend webhook coordination, EAS Build configuration for multiple profiles, and shared design token consumption from a monorepo package. Lower complexity than the backend services due to absence of database/infrastructure concerns, but the breadth of native features and cross-platform validation requirements keeps it at 7.

expansion_prompt: Break down Production Hardening into parallel work streams: (1) Replica scaling, HPA configuration for all 5 services, CNPG scale to 2 instances with WAL archival to R2, Valkey scale to 2 replicas, (2) CiliumNetworkPolicy authoring for all 6 service namespaces with egress allowlists for external APIs and deny-all defaults, (3) Cloudflare Tunnel deployment (cloudflared), tunnel routing configuration, HTTPS enforcement, and ServiceAccount/RBAC least-privilege hardening, (4) ArgoCD Application CRs for all services with automated sync, selfHeal, prune, health checks, and rollback alert rules, (5) CI/CD GitHub Actions pipeline (pr-review, quality, test, security, merge workflows) with Stitch/Cleo/Tess/Cipher/Atlas integrations, (6) Prometheus alerting rules (latency, error rate, pod down), Grafana dashboard definitions for all 5 services plus CNPG and Valkey, (7) GDPR end-to-end verification test: full lifecycle create → delete → audit log validation across all 5 services, (8) k6 performance SLO validation scripts (availability endpoint at 100 RPS, full quote flow, Morgan chat latency). Streams 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 can proceed in parallel. Stream 7 depends on all services being deployed (tasks 2-7). Stream 8 depends on stream 1 (replicas) and stream 2 (network policies must not block test traffic).
reasoning: Platform-wide hardening touching every service and infrastructure component: 5 HPAs, CNPG HA scaling with WAL archival, Valkey replication, 6 distinct CiliumNetworkPolicy sets with external API egress allowlists, Cloudflare Tunnel configuration, RBAC for every ServiceAccount, ArgoCD sync policies for all applications, a 5-workflow CI/CD pipeline integrating 5 different tooling agents, Prometheus alerting with Loki integration, Grafana dashboard definitions, cross-service GDPR end-to-end verification, and k6 performance load tests with SLO assertions. This is the only task that validates and depends on all other 9 tasks simultaneously.
